replication_study.hypothesis: When expected monetary returns are held constant in a threshold public-goods game, individual contributions (cont) will be lower when the bonus distribution includes potential losses (losses = 1) than when it does not (losses = 0).

data_plan.source_type: Laboratory / online experiment dataset exported from oTree
data_plan.wave_or_subset: All available sessions (SESSIONid 1–40) and four repeated rounds
data_plan.access_details: Unrestricted – file supplied in /app/data at execution time

qualification.explanation: The dataset comes from an independently run replication of the McCarter et al. (2010) threshold public-goods game, allowing us to re-test the focal loss-prospects hypothesis on new participants while keeping the task identical.
qualification.similarity_to_original:
• Uses the same 4-person threshold public-goods design described in the original paper (post_registration.json → method.description).
• Contains the identical outcome variable cont (dictionary.pdf, row: cont) and manipulation dummy losses (dictionary.pdf, row: losses), matching the focal claim in initial_details.txt.
qualification.deviation_from_original:
• Original studies had N≈62 & 65 undergraduate lab participants (post_registration.json → data.wave_or_subset); the replication dataset has ≈320 participants recruited both in-lab and via Prolific (dictionary.pdf, variable sessionconfigname shows "feele", "prolific").
• Original design used one decision per treatment; the replication records four repeated rounds (dictionary.pdf, variables CONT1-CONT4, round column), thus providing a larger but non-independent observation set.

data_plan.notes: Data include repeated measures; mixed-model specification will account for clustering at participant and session level. Variable names contain spaces in the csv filename but handled via Path. No missing values in key variables.

planned_method.steps:
1. Load dataset from /app/data.
2. Select columns cont (contribution), losses, id (participant), and sessioncode.
3. Drop rows with missing key variables (none expected).
4. Fit a linear mixed-effects model: cont ~ intercept + losses, with random intercepts for participants and a variance component for session.
5. Extract coefficient and p-value for losses.
6. Save results to /app/data/replication_results.csv.
planned_method.models: Linear mixed-effects model (random intercepts for participant; session as additional variance component).
planned_method.control_variables: Random intercepts implicitly control for unobserved participant and session heterogeneity.

planned_estimation_and_test.test: Two-sided Wald z-test from mixed-model output

planned_method.missing_data_handling: List-wise deletion on cont or losses (no missing values expected).
planned_method.inference_criteria: Reject H0 and support hypothesis if β_losses < 0 and p < 0.05 (two-tailed).

files.replicate_analysis.py: Loads the clean dataset, fits the mixed-effects model specified above, prints the summary, and writes key coefficients/p-values to replication_results.csv.

codebase.notes: All file IO hard-coded to /app/data to satisfy execution constraints.  Script entirely self-contained and uses only open-source Python libraries.

analysis.comparison_metrics: Sign and significance of β_losses compared with original F-test direction (negative effect).
